title: Call-In
text: Call-In Non-Uniface environments can call in to Uniface components. All communication is passed to the Uniface components, making it possible to create and delete instances of the Uniface components, invoke their operations, and pass data to and from the components. To be able to translate the communication between the different environments, the URB uses call-in mapping information that can be defined in the Signature Editor. This information is compiled into signatures. The non-Uniface environment is responsible for instantiating the Uniface component. Examples of call-in include Uniface services that are called from a Visual Basic component using COM, or from a Web services client using SOAP. Related Links Call-Out Uniface components can activate non-Uniface components, a process known as call-out. Signatures A Uniface signature describes the remote interface of a component. The interface enables other components to address the component without knowing anything about the implementation. This allows components of different origin to communicate through their respective interfaces. Signature Editor The Signature Editor enables you to define, edit, and compile component signatures for both Uniface and non-Uniface components. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
